speaker: interviewee
text: And it took me a while to learn the language, and to meet up with some girls. And as soon as I was happy with that, of course, my life became better. I went to school. And they wanted-- I made three grades. I mean, the three years that I-- I-- I was in DP camp, in [INAUDIBLE], I made seven grades, I should say. Everyone went their own speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had made-- I had-- I was seven-- I made seven grades. So they wanted to put me in junior high. But according to my age, I was already past that. So I wanted to be in 10th grade, something like that. But anyhow, I went to my mother to the-- to-- to high school. Because I met girls my age.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe I was 15 at the time. And-- and they were starting high school. And I wanted to start high school, too. So I went with my mother. And I remember it was Simon Gratz High School in Philadelphia. And the principal's name was Dr. Werner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was the first Holocaust survivor that he probably met, as a child. And-- and I-- and I explained to him, and I begged him, and I said, that I feel very confident that had a very wonderful education, even though I, you know, made only seven grades, but it was very extensive. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and if I cannot keep up with my grades-- because in those days, they had 10A and 10B. So I'll lose a half a year. It'll be my loss. And I'll go back to junior high. And for some reason, he must have taken pity on me, and he let me do it. And I did so well that I got an A in English. And I remember he announced my name. We had an auditorium one time. And he said, you kids, who were born and raised here in Philadelphia, you know, you can't even get this mark in English and here is a child, you know, who came-- so I-- I did OK. And so I stayed-- stayed, you know, in high school, graduated with my-- in '52, with all my friends. And-- but I kept telling my father, I'm going to Israel. I have not forgotten. I'm going back-- I'm going to Israel. And I was working part time. And I was saving money. I had $1,000 saved up. And I was going to go to Israel. So my father said, well, after you become a citizen, you can go to Israel. First, you have to become a citizen. Because you never know what's gonna happen. You have to-- first, you be a  citizen. So by the time I became a citizen, I met my husband. [LAUGHS] So that became of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Alex. And I met him on a blind date. And we were married in 1955. And my husband and my father went into business together in Levittown, PA, not far from Philadelphia, like, a half an hour away. They opened up a food market. And they-- they-- they got along like-- like a father and son. My parents adored my husband. He was a Holocaust survivor from Czechoslovakia, from Munkacs. And his parents and brothers and sisters were all killed, except for one brother. And so my parents automatically became his parents. And he adored them, and they adored him. It was really a love affair. And our business being together, so we were always together. We were very, very close. I started having my children. I have a daughter, Radie, and Susan, and Steven, born in '58, '60, and '66. And we were always together. And my-- and my aunts and uncles from Israel would come very often. And we would go there. My parents must have been in Israel the least half-- 12 times, maybe more than that. And so we were always together, with my cousins in Israel. I feel like they were my sisters. I just adored them. And I've been there many times. And I-- I had a wonderful life. And especially when it came holiday times. And I used to have 30 or 40 people for a Seder or for Rosh Hashanah. I knew that this couldn't last forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew how to appreciate that. And we made the most of it. And we'd sit around the table, especially when my uncle [INAUDIBLE] came from Israel. And they would be singing. All my neighbors would come in to hear.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was just beautiful. And it was great. I was very fortunate that I was able to meet such a wonderful person and have such great kids. And thank God, they were smart and did well in school and so forth. And but it didn't last. In '87, after my daughter got married-- my daughter got married in '85. But in '87, my husband died. And then two years later, my mother died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So things changed. But you have to make the most of it. And now I have two granddaughters that they're just the light of my life. And my daughter, Susan's, expecting very shortly, another baby. So we're all excited. And I spent a lot of time with them. And I have to take care of my father. He needs help lately.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm busy with that. And I was doing a little real estate. Although lately, I haven't done too much. Because whatever time I have, I like to spend it with my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think bringing up your children was affected by your experience as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think-- I have questioned my children whether they felt different from their friends. They said, no, they never did. For some reason or other, I think I brought them up the same way as my friends brought up their children. I'm a very protective mother. I mean, I'm crazy. I don't know whether that has anything to do with the experiences of the Holocaust, or it's my nature. I'm always worried and always check in with me. And they have to check with me. And if I don't hear from them, they're supposed to be a certain place, I go wild. I just go crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So maybe that has something to do with my experiences. I don't know. But we-- I'm very close to my kids. We have a very great relationship. And I'm their friend. That's nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was my friend. She was my very best friend, my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you like to leave for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I I believe that the Holocaust can happen again. We have to be on guard all the time. And because the German Jews were very snug in Germany. They thought they-- nothing could ever happen to them. I mean, they were Germans first and Jews second. And I guess Hitler showed them, you know, what was what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have to educate our children to be on guard, to belong to Jewish organizations, to be affiliated with synagogues, and above all, to support Israel in every way. Because a strong Israel is our-- is our survival. Without Israel, I-- I think we are really in danger. So that would be my message. In every-- in any way you possibly can, to support Israel, this is, like, number one. And be affiliated with all Jewish organizations, not to lose track of that. Part of your experience and your strength comes from your father. Would you like to tell me a little about him and introduce him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he's with us today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Well, my father's in his 90s. And he's been an inspiration to all of us all through the years. And we love him dearly. And lately, you know, he's getting on in years. And his health has not been that great. I mean, thank God. I'm not complaining for his age. And thank God that my brother, who is a physician, is keeping him alive, I think. If it wasn't for him, I don't know what would have happened to him. Because he's always on-- on top of the situation. And he's with the medication, and-- and he's really keeping him in shape. Every time something goes wrong, I ship him off to Dallas. And I said, send me back a better specimen. He does. So far, it has worked. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you like to introduce your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Isaak--

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaak Emmett.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's 94.

speaker: interviewer
text: Isaak Emmett. And can you tell me what your most difficult experience was during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I told you before, but I can't tell you [INAUDIBLE]. One of the most dangerous things was when I was in ghetto-- in ghetto. And my daughter, I think my daughter tell you-- I remember, and I know the child, Hannah, she was 10 years old. We don't [INAUDIBLE] keep life. Then we give my daughter to a man from-- Czech-- Czech man. He was the principal in school. He didn't have children. He-- he was the one for the best chance there. Then they came to us in-- in my house, arrest me, they said, everybody was already in-- what do you call it-- in the ghetto. And then she came. And she start crying and she tell, [INAUDIBLE]. And we decided we didn't have nothing. We did not even lose faith that [INAUDIBLE]. At least my daughter will be alive. And we give her to him. And she was with him almost a week before they kill the people, all of them. And every morning, my-- my-- my house, I have a-- my house was in the street. Because it was [INAUDIBLE] who lives there, the street was the name. At least in the right side, I was my house. Immediately was the way where you're going, [INAUDIBLE]. And the other side was the ghetto-- the ghetto. The place, the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fence was across my street. In this side was the ghetto. And on my side wasn't. And my house was in this. And my daughter, she used to come. She was sleeping there. And she used to come afternoon. And the-- and the gate was up. And you can't go in. You can't go out. Because not everything was open. And then she used to come to me in-- in the ghetto. And I used to talk to her. And she was with me a few hours. And she went back. This time was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this time I was sleeping not in the ghetto. I was sleeping in a friend's, my friend's. We were, maybe, about 10 people. Nighttime I'd say, we can speak in ghetto too. And I said, well, I'd better sleep over there. In the night, [INAUDIBLE] at about 4, 5 o'clock in the morning, they start knocking on the door. And they came and they took us, all of Lvov. And they take us all together. It was maybe a block, a good block, to the gates. The gate to the ghetto, from here. And I came in ghetto. And I see how I'm-- I'm-- I'm lost now. This is the last minute. Because usually it used to be open, the ghetto. And I want to see [INAUDIBLE]. And my Hannah stayed up. She come to my-- she's in ghetto. Then she-- she and [INAUDIBLE], that it's me. And I want to do something. And I don't know what. In the ghetto, the-- what was around the place--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fence. The fence. The fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fence was higher. Then I took-- I said, it's not necessary to say. I think I can't get over this place. Because it was very high.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you jump.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then saw my neighbor had a-- a-- a barrel, a higher barrel, for gas, for kerosene.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I pull it up a little bit. And I stand on this kerosene barrel. And here with me, is my sister, the older one, and everybody. And they say, don't go. The-- and this-- in the-- in the street, it's a Ukrainian [INAUDIBLE]. You're in [INAUDIBLE] with a rifle in the end. Again, if you-- if you not one in these streets. Because I crossed my street, my yard. And I-- and they start crying, and don't go. Because they kill you. They stay with you whenever. I say, I don't care if they kill me. I [? never-- ?] maybe-- maybe I can [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewee
text: Save.

speaker: interviewee
text: Save my daughter. And I was looking. And then men with the rifle was standing out on the other side. Then I jump over [INAUDIBLE] from the barrel, I was in the air, standing over the gate. And I was standing up. And I start running across the street from my house. And the man, where he was standing with the rifle. Here I said, and he start shooting it. One, two, three, four. And I heard. And I-- and I-- the puller-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The bullet.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bullet. [INAUDIBLE] to me. And I was over the other side. And when I-- and I went to my house, making sure this wasn't a problem. And then I run to my house. And I make-- can relax a little bit when I came in the corridor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hallway.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hallway.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hallway, yeah. And I try to be quiet. And I opened the door. And even was afraid that man, where he was shooting, he was afraid to go after me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because maybe some other different [INAUDIBLE] people could go jumping. And then I came in. I was relaxed a little bit when I came in corridor. How do you say corridor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hallway.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you were up. And I came up, and I knock on the door. And I came in. I said, good morning. And what are you doing here? They ask me. The woman, she was the wife for him, principal. I say, I want to say something. Where is Hannah? She said, she is still in bed. Then I was [INAUDIBLE] shorter. And I tell you what happened. And-- and-- and that's why he was jumping here. Because once you arrive, you don't go out now. Don't go out. And in my house, I have a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: A hiding spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever. And the [NON-ENGLISH] was-- and we came in the hallway, in corridor. How do say corridor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hallway.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hallway. It was once they came straight, on the right side, to the kitchen. And the left side was to the [NON-ENGLISH]. How do you say [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a shed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a shed. And this was maybe four steps from the top, and I was standing, coming in and out. They went down to my-- how do you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shed. And I supposed to go the four, five, steps. In this, the four or five steps, I make my [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Hiding spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: My hiding spot. And I used to turn up a little bit from the-- from the side. And I was going in. And I close it. I kept it straight. And nobody know. I'm-- I'm really into my house, in the ground. I was a few minutes. I think I said it earlier. And then this was my dangerous-- I was in dangerous all the times. 100 times, they was killing me, I was afraid. But this time, when they start shooting at me, and they shoot four, five, six times. And I was running across the street. This was a miracle they didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Catch you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --get me. And that's why I saved my daughter. And this was in a Monday. This was 1942. In-- and Wednesday, this was the Monday, the same week. And Wednesday, there was the [NON-ENGLISH], where they killed almost 4,000 or 5,000 people. They take [INAUDIBLE] to the graves, where they-- it was prepared. But for three, four graves, they brought engineers. They were measured up according to how many, too much people. What kind, how many graves they need, and what kind of big they supposed to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you say? What did you say, when they came in? The graves? Did you see the graves?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't see the graves. I can just tell you what it was. This time, on Monday, this was on a Monday, when I jump.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Wednesday, after, when I jump over that, you know, you can't go out from there-- from there-- from the ghetto. You can't come in. It was closed up, and around it was the police. And Wednesday, they ask everybody to go out. It was between 4,000 and 5,000, maybe 6,000 people. I don't know exactly because a lot of the people, [INAUDIBLE] they themselves in other [INAUDIBLE]. [NON-ENGLISH] and stand around in the kitchen. They-- when they saved his life. They didn't have anywhere to be more.

speaker: interviewer
text: They come the kitchen. Because the essentially, in this time, they done kill everybody. [INAUDIBLE] was the last one. Because the [INAUDIBLE] some factories from leather. How do you say leather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leather factories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leather-- they make leather factories. They wanted to save the factories. So then they-- yeah. Then they keep [INAUDIBLE] switching the last one, and they didn't kill her. Anyway, I don't know whether you want to ask something more.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you what-- what your message would be for future generations? What kind of wisdom can you tell future generations about what you have experienced? What is your message to society?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw that they'll kill everybody. Because you can't trust them. When after that, they kill all the people, it was on a Wednesday, I told you. Thursday, they say, this is free. Everybody no more, killing Jews. You can go. You can do anything you want to. And I was in my [INAUDIBLE] in my grave, when they killed [INAUDIBLE]. This time instead of killing, 400, 500 houses, starting burning in the one same moment. Because they saw the president, [? Schwarzmann, ?] he said, no I can't tell you nothing. Do whatever you want. This is the end. And he kill himself. And then started, gave everybody a little bit kerosene, a little bit straw. He burning up himself. he start burning at one time, 400, 500 houses in the ghetto. And I was laying down in my grave. And I-- And I heard every shot. And my house was across the street from-- it was [? Schwarzmann ?] go something. It was not in my house. It was in the other side. And it was the wind on this side. It was burning up, not question, because there was a very big fire. And the whole ghetto was in there, across the street, not on my side where I was. And after that, and I'm laying down in the grave. I don't know what to do now. And they left and they killed-- there were people. They killed a lot of people. And not the graves, before the graves. Because they started saying, go! So they took them near the synagogues. It was a very big place. Then they start running. And some maybe, I don't know exactly, 20, 30 people was killed with the training. Maybe 30 or 40, 50 children was killed. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what I want to say? I want to come up to something. You asked me something. What message would you like to leave for future--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The message-- what do you want to tell people?

speaker: interviewee
text: One moment, just this I must tell you. Second day, after they kill, all of a sudden they say, no more killing. You just can go any place and anyone to. And I was-- my daughter, [INAUDIBLE], and my farmer, my river. When I-- when I hear this, then I came up already, and I tell them that I am here. And the people, they-- they-- they keep my daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell you-- and I tell you, you know what? I'm going to [INAUDIBLE], where [INAUDIBLE]. If tell you what to do, and I went. All of a sudden, I went. Nobody touched me. They didn't do nothing. And they give our house, one house. It's a [INAUDIBLE]. He calls me. Then he tell me he was [INAUDIBLE]. He said, everybody don't have place. Maybe he was [NON-ENGLISH]. Maybe-- I don't know if you know what [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Forest. And then they can go in this and this house. Everybody, nobody touched him. And they came in, about 300, 400, 500 people. What they were hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this moment, and they don't kill. I can go out myself. And I was out and I didn't believe myself. Even if I have it. Then I saw him. [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH], a shoemaker, I say him running. Because there was-- from my secret place, I have some open places I can look in the street. And not-- not too much, not in a way like this, to matter. Then I saw him working.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. Then I went-- I-- I decided to go to see my wife with my daughter through this power. And I went, and nobody touched me. And I went straight. And I came to that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Laura had told us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just we stay here a few days. As we [INAUDIBLE]. Because they think now, they kill all the Jews. Nobody-- nobody they were killing. And this [INAUDIBLE], [? the want to ?] [? arrest ?] 400 or 500 people when they came from the graves, when they save them self. They was ready. And I think [INAUDIBLE]. And three-- and three days later, they turn around-- they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They did it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: They did it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they surrounded them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Laura, do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took them up, and they kill them all. [INAUDIBLE] cemetery, the Jewish cemetery, took him to the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Laura, what would you like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1991, I joined a group. There was a symposium in New York City, Children of the Holocaust was a new-formed group. Because the children of the Holocaust said that the survivors, the Holocaust survivors did not--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, we have to change your microphone. One second here. Would you like to add anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes. In 1991, I joined a group, Children of the Holocaust. There was a newly-formed group. And the reason for that is the children of the Holocaust felt that their voice was not being heard. That the survivors said we didn't remember, and we didn't know how-- we can't contribute anything. But it wasn't so. We remembered a lot. So we formed our own group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York City. It was the first symposium they've had. Since in Israel, they've gotten together, in Los Angeles. So it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first one was in New York City. And I went. And it was very interesting. We had a lot of workshops. And what I saw, mostly, was a lot of-- well, there were people in their late 50s. I guess they were, you know, young, under 10 years old. And they were-- they survived the war on their own, mostly in hiding places as Christians. A lot of them did not want to be Jewish. They wanted to remain as a Christian. They had to be kidnapped many times and forced into Judaism again. But quite a few of them, they-- they were-- I noticed that a large percentage of them was-- they were educated. But they could not have a personal relationship. They were divorced. A great majority were divorced. And they felt very insecure. They didn't trust anybody. And I attribute that because they were separated from their parents. I didn't have that experience. I was lucky because I was with my parents. So that played a big role, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now I want to see some pictures of your grandchildren.
